---
title: "When dense retrieval quietly fails at K=10"
description: "On a 4-hop question over 21k chunks, dense retrieval delivers one gold chunk of four; the graph walk pins the bridge chunks it misses. Retrieval is envelope assembly, not question answering."
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [retrieval]
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Status"}
**Built demonstration, with an honest boundary.** The dense-retrieval failure numbers are measured on MuSiQue. The competing graph method's win at 21k scale is *not* yet demonstrated; it is deferred on compute and is not projected here.
:::

**Thesis.** Retrieval is not about answering a question. It is about assembling a context envelope, the local neighborhood of concepts, layout, and cross-document evidence the model needs. When the envelope is missing, the model cannot answer, even when the strictly correct chunk sits in the top-K.

The measured part is stark. On MuSiQue at 21,000 chunks, at the production cutoff of K equals 10, dense retrieval delivers the single direct-match chunk for a question and effectively nothing else. For a four-hop question, the model gets one gold chunk of the four it needs. The deepest four-hop chunk sits at a median rank of 1,796 out of 21,100, essentially never surfaced. Read as effective multi-hop retrieval, it collapses to 17 percent at two hops, and 0 percent at three and four. Those are measurements, not projections.

The framing that explains it: there are two kinds of hop. A reasoning hop lives on the question side and can be helped by decomposing the question. A retrieval hop lives on the index side, where the envelope actually is, and decomposition does not help you there. Multi-hop scientific questions are retrieval-hop-heavy by construction, which is why this whole line of work is scoped to scientific research rather than general question answering.

Now the boundary, stated as plainly as the site itself states it. Everything above is the *dense-failure* side, and it is measured. The claim that a graph-walk method fixes it at the same 21,000-chunk scale is **not yet demonstrated**. That comparison is incomplete, blocked on a distillation step estimated at about 300 dollars in compute, and the site does not project the walk's win at scale. The dense method is shown to fail; the alternative is shown to be promising at small scale and honestly marked as unproven at large scale.

The line worth keeping: when the envelope is missing, the language model cannot answer, even when the strictly correct chunk is in the top-K.
